nativeRange.habitat: Tropical West and Central African freshwater rivers and streams, particularly in the lowland forest belt of Cameroon, where it grows rooted in fine substrate along clear flowing watercourses

decorPreferences: fine gravel or nutrient-rich substrate (avoid coarse or heavy gravel)
open background space tall enough for long arching leaves
leave at least the top half of the bulb exposed above the substrate

goodWith: plant-nibbling fish (tough leaves are generally ignored by herbivores)

summary: The African Onion Plant (Crinum calamistratum) is a slow-growing West African bulb plant known for its very long, narrow, deeply curled dark-green leaves, suited to the background or centrepiece of medium-to-large planted aquariums.
careNotes:
Crinum calamistratum, commonly known as the African Onion Plant or Curly Crinum, is a West African freshwater bulb plant from the family Amaryllidaceae, instantly recognisable by its very long, narrow, deeply crinkled or curly dark-green leaves. Leaves typically reach around 40 cm in juvenile plants and can extend to 100–120 cm in well-established specimens, so this plant is best positioned at the back of medium-to-large aquariums where its arching foliage has room to develop.

PLANTING IS CRITICAL: the onion-shaped bulb must NEVER be fully buried. Only the bottom half to two-thirds of the bulb should sit in the substrate, with the upper portion remaining exposed above it; burying the whole bulb causes it to rot. A fine, nutrient-rich gravel substrate is preferred — heavy or coarse gravel and very deep sand are not recommended — and supplementing with root tabs is advisable, as this species is a heavy root feeder.

Light requirements are reported variably in the hobby: one prominent supplier lists the demand as low, while most aquarium retailers and care guides recommend medium-to-high lighting for healthy growth and the possibility of flowering. A medium level is a reasonable middle ground, and the species appears to tolerate a fairly wide range. CO2 supplementation is optional rather than required.

Crinum calamistratum is slow to establish and reacts poorly to transplantation and sudden shifts in water chemistry — once settled, it is best left undisturbed. Its leaves are tough and generally ignored by plant-eating fish such as goldfish and large cichlids, making it one of the few large plants that can work in tanks with herbivorous species. The plant also has some tolerance for low brackish salt concentrations, though it is primarily kept as a freshwater species.
breedingNotes: Crinum calamistratum propagates vegetatively through small daughter bulbs (sometimes called bulb shoots) that develop from the base of an established mother bulb. These can be separated and replanted once they have developed their own roots and leaves. Mature plants kept under strong lighting may produce white flowers on tall emergent stalks, though flowering is uncommon in typical aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica species page for Crinum calamistratum. Origin West Africa, height 40–120 cm, light demand low, CO2 demand low, growth rate slow, dark-green narrow curly leaves, forms small bulbs, tolerates low brackish salinity, resistant to herbivorous fish. additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed bulb plant with very long, narrow, dark-green crinkly leaves; extremely slow growing; background or centerpiece placement; at least medium lighting; easy/beginner-friendly care designation.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Used for water parameters where Tropica is silent: temperature 20–27 °C, pH 6.1–7.8, hardness 0–18 dGH. Confirmed height 40–120 cm, slow growth, family Amaryllidaceae, origin Cameroon, propagation by basal bulb shoots, leave part of bulb exposed to prevent rot, medium difficulty.

additional.name: Aqualand Pets Plus — Crinum Calamistratum
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 20–28 °C (68–82 °F), pH 6.1–7.9, explicit half-bury rule (only ~2/3 of bulb in substrate, ~1/3 exposed; fully buried = rot), fine gravel substrate, heavy root feeder, medium-to-high lighting, poor transplantation tolerance.

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics — Crinum Calamistratum
additional.notes: Used for temperature 23–26 °C, lighting 8–10 hours per day moderate-to-high, slow growth, leaves up to ~4 ft / ~120 cm, native to west/central Africa especially Cameroon, generally ignored by plant-eating fish, can flower under bright light in soft slightly acidic water.
